planting.note: Divide established clumps in early spring and replant divisions immediately into consistently moist to waterlogged soil in partial shade; the golden color intensifies with some direct morning sun but bleaches to pale chartreuse in deep shade or harsh afternoon light.

directions: Divide established clumps in early spring or fall and replant immediately in consistently moist to waterlogged soil at pond edges, bog gardens, or perpetually damp borders in partial shade; Bowles' golden sedge produces arching tufts of brilliant gold-yellow leaves edged with a thin green margin that illuminate shaded spots with a warm, luminous glow all season. Color is most intense in positions with morning sun and adequate moisture, and may bleach in deep shade or prolonged drought. Cut back any tatty foliage in late winter before new growth emerges. An excellent container subject when kept standing in a saucer of water.
sources: North Carolina State Extension Gardener Plant Toolbox

planting.note: Plant divisions in early spring in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic, well-drained soil in shade to dappled light; tolerates heavier moisture than many wood ferns and spreads slowly by short rhizomes to form a weed-suppressing colony.

directions: Plant divisions in early spring in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic, well-drained soil in full shade to dappled woodland light; the spreading wood fern produces broad, arching, triangular-ovate fronds on dark stalks, forming graceful mounds that soften woodland garden paths and shaded borders with fresh spring-green color that deepens to rich forest green through summer. Semi-deciduous to deciduous, it spreads gradually by short rhizomes to form a natural-looking weed-suppressing colony. Tolerates heavier moisture than many wood ferns and establishes readily in the humus-rich leaf-mold soils typical of mature deciduous woodland.

planting.note: Plant container-grown divisions in spring or fall in well-drained, humus-rich, slightly acidic soil in shade or bright indirect light; avoid waterlogged soils — good drainage paired with consistent moisture suits it best.

directions: Plant container-grown divisions in spring or fall in well-drained, humus-rich, slightly acidic soil in shade or bright indirect light; tassel fern earns its name from the distinctive golden-bronze, tassel-like croziers that unfurl in early spring before expanding into glossy, lustrous, deep evergreen fronds — one of the most refined and elegant shade ferns for temperate gardens, with outstanding year-round presence. Avoid waterlogged soils; good drainage paired with consistent moisture is ideal. Slugs can damage emerging croziers severely in spring — early protection is worthwhile.

common_name: Meadow Blazing Star (Rocky Mountain Blazing Star)

planting.note: Surface-sow or lightly cover seed in fall for natural cold stratification over winter; plants grow from corms and typically bloom the second year from seed — transplant corms in spring for first-year bloom.

directions: Surface-sow seed in fall to receive natural cold stratification over winter, or cold-stratify for 60 days then transplant corms in spring; meadow blazing star is widely documented as the single most attractive native plant for monarch butterflies during their southward fall migration — densely blooming patches can draw dozens of monarchs during peak flight periods. Stout upright stems bear spikes of rosy-purple flowerheads that open from the top down through August and September, and the nectar-rich blooms are magnets for swallowtails and bumblebees as well. Grows from a fleshy corm that becomes fully drought-tolerant once established; allow seed heads to stand through winter to feed goldfinches.

planting.note: Sow seed in fall for natural cold stratification, or cold-stratify for 60–90 days before spring sowing; division of established clumps in early spring is the most reliable propagation method.

directions: Sow seed in fall for natural cold stratification, or divide established clumps in early spring, planting in moist but well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial shade to dappled woodland light; American ipecac is an airy native perennial closely related to Gillenia trifoliata, producing clouds of small white five-petaled star flowers on reddish branching stems in late spring to early summer, followed by attractive reddish seed capsules. The foliage turns orange-red in fall and the persistent reddish calyces add winter interest. An excellent naturalistic garden plant that blends beautifully with hostas, ferns, and other shade-tolerant companions, and makes a delicate cut flower.
